contents: The workman 2a-m-Nwn files a complaint in the onb.t against watercarrier Pn-Niw.t , who has taken his donkey and its young, which both died. After that 2a-m-Nwn has lodged four complaints, which have all been granted, but he has not been recompensed by Pn-Niw.t . The latter is punished and swears he will pay 2a-m-Nwn . After three weeks he brings 2a-m-Nwn a small amount of grain.

The reverse, which may be connected, mentions the arrival of an army officer, the sr.w and the gang, a chapel (?) and 6A-Sri .
terminology: mn (rev. 4 (?)); hrw pn (rev. 6); hrw pn n ... (rev. 5); see Remarks; [...] (?) spr in NN (rev. ~ 2 # ); di.w (obv. 3)

remarks: Keywords: in rev. 5 mention may be made of a 'chapel' ( xnw ), or it may be part of a strange writing of 'in the Necropolis' ( m pA 2r ). Terminology: the word left unread by Černý in hrw pn n ... could possibly be ii 'arrive'. Date attributed: the names suggest a dating in the reign of Ramesses III or Ramesses IV (DvH).

remarks: Description: the hight is given as 20 cm and the width as 13.3 cm by Grandet in: Les artistes de Pharaon (2002), 206 no. 157. However, the photographs show that the measurements must be hight 13.5 cm and width 20 cm (J.T.-V.). A curving line probably in black ink separates the bottom lines on obverse starting from the end of obverse 6, from the lines above (obverse 1-middle of obverse 6). The end of the line numbered by Hieratische Papyrus Berlin III, pl. XXXIV, XXXIVa as obverse 6 is numbered by Kitchen as obverse 7, whereby the total number of lines on obverse becomes 10. Kitchen, Ramesside Inscriptions VI, 164. Content(s): The same indiviuals feature in a similar transfer recorded in O. Ashmolean Museum 0136. See van Walsem, in: Deir el-Medina in the Third Millenium AD , 348, 349. Names, Titles: The reading Mw.t-Mw.t (obv. 2) by Toivari-Viitala, Women at Deir el-Medina , 128. The alternative reading "mother" (obv. 2) by Janssen, Commodity Prices , 26. The emendation "his mother?" (obv. 2) is suggested by McDowell, Village Life , 81. The reading sS-od 1r-Sri (rev. 2) by Janssen, Commodity Prices , 26.

contents: On obverse first an account of a payment made by workman Imn-wa to carpenter PA-mdw-nxt for making a bed. In the last lines (in a different hand) a dated note about a dispute between the two parties concerning some outstanding payment. On reverse first an account concerning the payment of a bed (the same as on obverse?) - the items given include a jackal, followed by a note (in a different hand) recording that the scribe of the Necropolis administered an oath on the two parties concerning the transaction.

contents: A protocol concerning an anonymous woman's unpaid debt. After the woman fails to pay the 20 deben copper, sentenced by the onb.t , she is taken to the onb.t of the Ramesseum, where this previous sum is raised to 30 deben copper. She takes an oath, but eventually pays only a part of the required amount, and a water-carrier is summoned to go and seize the remaining debt and fine from her.

dates_attributed: Dyn. 20, year 21 Ramesses III (Allam, Blackman, Demarée, Erman, Helck, Kitchen, McDowell); year 21 and year 22 Ramesses III (Grandet)
contents: Statement by an unnamed person about the assignment of a tomb to his ancestor 1Ay in regnal year 7 of King Horemheb. Through an oracle of Amenhotep I, the same tomb was given to the speaker on II Smw of year 21 (of Ramesses III), and he began working in it. Almost a year later, on I Smw 7, the scribe Imn-nxt points out that his tomb gives access to the nearby tomb of the workman 2a-m-Nwn . See Remarks.
terminology: m bAH Imn-Htp anx wDA snb (obv. 5); mDA.t (obv. 7; in m mDA.t ); hrw n sao i.ir NN r pA 2r (obv. 1-2)
names_titles: an. ( Pr-aA anx wDA snb ; king; obv. 3); Imn (for Imn-ms ? obv. 3; see Remarks); Imn-nxt ( sS ; rev. 6); Imn-Htp anx wDA snb (deity; obv. 5); BAk-n-wrnr ( rmT-is.t ; rev. 5-6); Nfr-Htp ( AT.w ; rev. 4); 1Ay ( rmT-is.t ; it of speaker; obv. 1-2 - written rmT , 3 - no title, 6 - no title); 1nr ( mw.t of speaker; Sri.t of 1Ay ; f.; obv. 3- ~ 4 # ); 1r sA 1y-nfr (rev. 5); 2a-m-Nwn ( rmT-is.t ; rev. 1, 7 - no title); 2nsw ( aA n is.t ; rev. 4); 9Hwty-ms ( aA n pr n Niw.t ; obv. 2); 9sr-xpr.w-Ra anx wDA snb 1r-m-Hb anx wDA snb (king; obv. 1)
remarks: Description, Publication: the 'recto' of Blackman, Allam, Hari, Helck and Kitchen is actually the reverse (and their 'verso' the obverse), since the text starts with the regnal year of Horemheb (as was assumed by Erman); see McDowell, Village Life , 69: in regnal years of Ramesses III, I Smw sw 6 (rev. 2) was later, not earlier, than II Smw sw 1 (obv. 5). Contents: text related to O. Florence 2621 and P. Berlin P 10496. Names, Titles: emendation Imn-ms in obv. 3 based on P. Berlin P 10496 (see Blackman, JEA 12 (1926), 177, note 11 and other authors).

contents: Obverse and edge: letter by an anonymous speaker ( a 'native of the Necropolis') about copper tools and other valuable objects hidden in the house of another (= Ms , obv. 7?). The objects had been missing for some time, and servants of the speaker's family had been seized on account of this. The speaker intends to take the objects to the overseer of the royal treasury, and have the servants released. Reverse: beginning of a wisdom text similar to the 'Prohibitions' (see Remarks).

contents: The lady 1r.y-iA is accused before the court ( onb.t ) by the workman Nb-nfr of having stolen a copper spike from him. 1r.y-iA has been denounced to Nb-nfr by the lady Nbw-m-nHb.t , who has experienced a divine manifestation to the effect. 1r.y-iA swears that she has not stolen the spike, but when a messenger of the court is sent to her house, he finds the spike there, hidden together with a metal object belonging to "Amun of the Beautiful Encounter". 1r.y-iA is deemed guilty and "worthy of death", but the case is postponed until the vizier will arrive. After the list of court members there follows a message by an anonymous writer to his superior concerning the earlier theft of a metal object by 6A-nDm.t-Hms , the wife of PA-Sd , who was consequently taken to the river-bank by the vizier. That case is now adduced as a precedent of the above one, and the addressee (the vizier?) is urged to take the appropriate measures.

contents: The workman Nb-nfr accuses his colleague 1y of having stolen 3 spikes belonging to Pharaoh. Two other workmen, who are said by Nb-nfr to have been witnesses to this deed, are brought into court; they swear that they have seen two spikes in the hut of 1y but they do not know anything more about the matter. Nb-nfr takes an oath saying that the spikes in question belong to Pharaoh, and also that 1y has ordered a cauldron to be made from stolen copper, and traded it for two donkeys. However, 1y himself takes an oath saying that the spikes in his possession are his own property. A list of court members follows. See Remarks.

remarks: Provenance: see Remarks on O. BMFA 72.666. Ryholt assumes the ostracon to derive from "another Theban source" than Deir el-Medina, since the name he read is unknown from that community. Dates attributed: in spite of what Ryholt thinks, the palaeography points to dyn. 19. Contents: according to Ryholt this type of text was used for oracle petitions, but administrative purposes are equally possible. Names, Titles: Ryholt incorrectly reads the name as 9Hwty-Hr-maw. For a similar defective writing of the name see O. IFAO 1060.

remarks: Provenance: bought at the auction of the Robert J. Hay collection in 1868 by C. Granville Way and in 1872 donated by his son to the Boston Museum of Fine Arts. Ryholt incorrectly states that the High Priest of Amun did not play a role in the workmen's community and therefore assumes the ostracon to derive from another Theban source than Deir el-Medina. Contents: Ryholt translates: "... who will place us above", but this seems unlikely. For a similar oracle question see O. IFAO 0849.

remarks: Description, Publication: based on Cern Notebook 34.6-9. The line numbering there is not the same as in Kitchen, Ramesside Inscriptions VII ; the latter's numbers are retained here in all fields except Description. Key: Kitchen obv. 1-11 = Cern obv. 1-15; Kitchen obv. 12-14 = obv. II 16-18; Kitchen obv. 15-28 = obv. III 1-14; Kitchen rev. 7-20 = rev. I 2-15; Kitchen rev. 21-41 = rev. II 1-25; Kitchen rev. 42-46 = rev. III 3-9. Provenance: according to Speleers, Recueil des inscriptions , 48 . Dates mentioned: Cern Notebook 34.6 reads IV Smw sw 25 instead of III Smw sw 15 in obv. 20 ( Kitchen, Ramesside Inscriptions VII, 357 ), and IV Smw sw 26 ( Cern Notebook 34.9 ) instead of IV Smw sw 25 ( Kitchen, Ramesside Inscriptions VII, 358 ) in rev. 36. Content: "joins Gardiner ostr. 20? cf. also ostr. Gard. 14." (Cern Notebook 34.6). Terminology: read dmD s 25 instead of ir n 21 in rev. 35 ( Cern Notebook 34.9 )? Names, Titles: instead of Pn-6A-wr.t (obv. 17; Kitchen, Ramesside Inscriptions VII, 357 ), Cern Notebook 34.6, reads Pn-Rnnw.t . 2a (obv. 19) read by Cern Notebook 34.6. In obv. 22 read idn.w 1Ay (Cern Notebook 34.7). In obv. 26 read Imn-nxt sA Imn-m-Ip.t (Cern Notebook 34.7)? In obv. 27 read aA-nxt (Cern Notebook 34.7). In obv. 28 read Nb-nxt (Cern Notebook 34.7). In rev. 22 read Nfr-Htp sA In-Hr-xa (Cern Notebook 34.8). In rev. 24 read PA-wr-aA (Cern Notebook 34.8). In rev. 25 read 6A-Sri (Cern Notebook 34.8). In rev. 40-41 read ... Ra-mry sA 1r OH.y (Cern Notebook 34.9). Text on edge (lines 2 and 3) not in Kitchen, Ramesside Inscriptions VII ; see Cern Notebook 34.6.

publication: Allam, Hieratische Ostraka und Papyri , 53 and 54 (no. 25), pls. 24-27 (description, transcription, photograph, translation, commentary); Kitchen, Ramesside Inscriptions IV, 230 and 231 (transcription) ; VII, 231 (transcription of obv. 1-3 and beginnings of obv. 4-10; see Remarks)
contents: Obverse: list of items held by the workman 1y , his wife Ii and his daughter-in-law, all or some of which have been received from PA-TAw-m-di-Imn . Report has been made about this by the speaker to the chief workman 1Ay , who said that report should be made to the court ( onb.t ). Reverse: the father(?) of the speaker (otherwise of 1y , who might be a different person) has died during the war. The commodities he has left include objects of wood, which are to be used to produce a statue for PA-TAw-m-di-Imn . The speaker has also made some garments for PA-TAw-m-di-Imn from yarn procured by the latter. Rev. 11 and 12 mention a transaction between PA-TAw-m-di-Imn and 2Am.y , in which 1y is also involved.
terminology: ir n (rev. 10); aHa (rev. 6 - red/erased, 7 - red); m Dr.t NN (obv. 10); nty m a NN (obv. 11); smi (obv. 12, 13); di=tw rx=tw (rev. 3; for di.t rx=tw ?) ; di=tw rx=tw ix.t nty m a NN (obv. 1; di=tw rx=tw for di.t rx=tw ?);

Cairo CG. 25227 originates from this particular Deir el-Medina excavation, especially on account of the proper names (Toivari-Viitala).

Names, Titles: MnTw-r-HA.t=f (obv. ~ 2-3 # ) was initially read MnTw-Hr-HA.t=f by Černý, BiOr 19 (1962), 142a, 143b. The name is refered to as MnTw-HA.t=f in Černý, Community of Workmen , 143. The form MnTw-r-HA.t=f was proven the correct reading by Janssen, JEA 53 (1967), 163-164. After having first thought to have discerned the name 2nsw-m-Hb (obv. 8) written in very pale ink, 2nsw-ms appeared to be a more likely reading to Černý, Community of Workmen , 168 note 1.

description: Limestone, 27 x 40 cms. Damaged (numerous pieces). Two sides. Obverse: 7 colums in black ink, of 28, 31, 27, 30, 31, 26 and 25 lines. Col. VII 20-25 in red ink. Reverse: six columns numbered I-III- IV a, V-VI, of 18, 28, 29, 11, 16 and 14 lines. Above col. IV a - VI there is a col. IV of 5 lines. Below cols IV-VI there is 1 line written upside down. Damage occurs in tops of obverse cols I-III and in the middle of col. V; on reverse the lower halfs of IV a and V-VI are much mutilated, as is the left side of col. IV. Correction by the scribe in obverse I 1, 17, III 25, IV 2, 5, V 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Strikethrough by the scribe in obverse I 6, 16, II 4, III 4, above V 13, reverse, II 15, 15, 17, IV a 11. A line divides the heading from cols V-VI on the reverse.

description: Limestone, 43 x 25 cms. Damaged (numerous pieces). Two sides. Obverse: 28 lines in black ink. Reverse: 28 lines in black ink. On the obverse most damage occurs in the lower half of the text, where the endings of the lines and in some cases also the middle and beginning are affected. Damage to the reverse is also extensive, most lines being affected (e.g. the beginnings of l. 11-20, the middle and/or endings of l. 4-8 and 10-17. Corrections by the scribe in obverse, 6, 9, 11, 15, 17, 23, reverse. Signs or groups crossed out by the scribe in obverse, 8, 12, 14, 14, above 21, above 27, below 28.

other_nos: O. Cairo JE 51514; O. Cairo SR 01448
description: Limestone, 23 x 20 cms. Damaged (numerous pieces). Two sides. Palimpsest: obverse shows traces of three older texts: alpha 5 battered lines of an account; beta 3 lines of a literary text (presumably), and gamma text of 23 short lines. Text beta and gamma were written in red ink and erased, lending the surface a reddish appearance. The definite text on obverse ( delta ) is in 15 lines in black ink, the last line being partly in red ink. Reverse: traces of older text visible. Over these: 22 lines in black ink in a uncial hand. Text gamma shows lacunas in l. 7-9, 19 and 21-23, delta in l. 3, 5-10, the reverse very small lacunas in about half of the lines. Correction by the scribe in delta 4, 11, 14 and reverse 1.

other_nos: O. Cairo JE 96130; O. Cairo SR 01277
description: Limestone, 27 x 15.5 cms. Damaged. One side, with two columns of 23 and 14 lines. Black ink except l. 36 which is in red ink. L. 23 has broken off the ostracon; perhaps more lines have broken off at this spot. Vertical traits and dots occur preceding or after many lines. From l. 3-21 a vertical stroke runs to the right of the lines which was erased in antiquity, as were the dots preceding the lines. Many lines are preceded or followed by oblique strokes (engraved), possibly checking marks. Line 37 is also engraved and not in ink.
provenance: Valley of Kings; Davis/Ayrton excavations 1905-1906; mark: N.Tb. 52 (KV 47 Siptah); see Reeves, Valley of the Kings , 108, 113 n. 51, 114 n. 56, 259, 263 n. 92 and 298.
